title-group.article-title: Study of Chemosensory Enhancement through Neuromodulation Training (SCENT): Design and methodology of a randomized clinical trial for COVID-related persistent smell dysfunction☆

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences, Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, SC, USA
bRalph H. Johnson Veterans Affairs Medical Center, Charleston, SC, USA
cDepartment of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery, Medical University of South Carolina, Charleston, SC, USA

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author. Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences Medical University of South Carolina Charleston, SC, 29425, USA. corteseb@musc.edu

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Few evidence-based treatments exist for COVID-related persistent smell dysfunction. While smell/olfactory training (ST) has emerged as a widely prescribed, first line treatment, rigorous study is required to determine its efficacy in Long COVID. Additional study and development of adjunctive methods to improve the efficacy of ST is also needed.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This paper details the study design and methodology for a large, at-home, randomized, controlled trial designed to determine whether ST and/or trigeminal nerve stimulation (TNS)-enhanced ST improves Long COVID-related disturbances in smell function, mood, sleep, and cognition. Adults with COVID-related persistent smell dysfunction (N = 180) will be recruited and randomized to self-administer ST, placebo smell training (PBO), or TNS-enhanced ST daily for 12 weeks. Our primary objectives are to i) determine the efficacy of ST, compared to any natural gain in function, on olfactory-specific deficits, ii) determine the TNS-enhanced effects of ST on olfactory-specific deficits, and iii) determine if TNS-enhanced ST, compared to ST, is also more efficacious in the treatment of other symptoms of Long COVID.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Post COVID persistent smell dysfunction and related deficits are relatively common with very few treatment options. Our proposed study will lay the groundwork for further development of ST and TNS as evidence-based treatments for Long COVID.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Post-acute sequelae of severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-coV-2), more commonly known as Long COVID, is defined by the World Health Organization as a condition occurring in individuals with a history of probable or confirmed SARS-coV-2 infection that i) typically develops 3 months from the onset of the initial symptoms, ii) lasts for at least 2 months, and iii) cannot be explained by an alternative diagnosis. Symptoms of Long COVID include the onset of new psychiatric problems including increased anxiety and depression, cognitive impairment, often referred to as “brain fog”, as well as sleep dysfunction and daytime fatigue [[1], [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7]]. Reportedly, as high as 54 % of people recovered from the initial infection endorse at least 1 long-term (>6 month from diagnosis) symptom of Long COVID [8].

While sudden smell loss gained widespread recognition in the early days of the pandemic as a hallmark feature of the SARS-coV-2 infection [[9], [10], [11]], smell dysfunction has also emerged as a core symptom of Long COVID, as it frequently persists well past the initial recovery [12,13]. Rates of unresolved COVID-related total smell loss, known as anosmia, measure around 21 % [14], with unresolved hyposmia, a reduction in smell function, or parosmia, a distorted sense of smell even higher, at nearly 50 % [15,16]. Smell dysfunction, regardless of its etiology, can also lead to numerous secondary neuropsychiatric impairments in mood, cognition, and sleep [[17], [18], [19], [20], [21], [22], [23], [24]]. Given that these same impairments in mood, cognition, and sleep are being recognized in the symptomatology of Long COVID [[1], [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7]], the possibility exists that smell dysfunction is both a symptom of Long COVID, as well as a contributing factor that worsens the other symptoms of Long COVID (e.g., mood, cognition, sleep, etc.). Moreover, if smell dysfunction contributes to other Long COVID neuropsychiatric problems, then the possibility also exists that successful treatment of smell dysfunction may be critical to overall improvement of Long COVID.

The societal impact of COVID-related smell dysfunction is exacerbated by the fact that few interventions exist to treat it. Smell/olfactory training (ST) has emerged as the first line, and only (current) treatment for COVID-related smell dysfunction [[25], [26], [27], [28]]. Classic ST [29] requires twice daily practice of sniffing various odor cues while engaging odor-related cognitive processes. Presumably, this daily practice employs the entire olfactory circuit, from receptors in the nasal epithelium to the olfactory orbitofrontal cortex, a circuit required for the full smell experience including the perception of odor features like hedonic valence and intensity, as well as the feelings, memories and other cognitions specific to that odor. Three or more months of daily ST is believed to engage the regenerative capacity of the olfactory circuit [30] and the top-down, cognitive processes that facilitate learning-induced plasticity [31] that are required to “retrain” the brain to smell. While ST is promising as a stand-alone treatment, it is not without limitations. Most noteworthy are patient burden of many months of daily practice and modest effect sizes. Thus, various methods to improve the efficacy of ST are currently being studied, including extending the training duration to 6 months [32], increasing the number of training odors or daily training sessions [[33], [34], [35]], and employing well-controlled training during daily clinic visits [36]. While some of these enhanced methods demonstrate gains in function, all modifications further increase patient burden. Thus, the development of methods to enhance the effects of ST without increasing burden, for example accelerating the time to reach a therapeutic response, are essential to improving the current standard of care.

Central to the present study, the trigeminal nerve and its extended neurocircuit are integral to olfactory function, operating in conjunction with the olfactory nerve and neurocircuit to mediate odor perception [[37], [38], [39], [40], [41], [42], [43]]. This olfactory-trigeminal functional coupling is grounded in the structural co-localization of olfactory receptors and the sensory endings of trigeminal fibers in the nasal epithelium [37]. Although the olfactory receptors and trigeminal fibers convey different information from odorants through separate neurocircuits with distinct roles, widespread central convergence allows for mutual influence of these two systems [[38], [39], [40], [41], [42], [43]]. Given this influence, it is reasonable to question whether the trigeminal system can be leveraged to activate or enhance sense of smell and whether a method that directly engages the intranasal trigeminal system may be an effective strategy to treat smell dysfunction.

Trigeminal nerve stimulation (TNS) is a novel, non-invasive, pain-free, method of neuromodulation currently being used to reduce symptoms associated with a wide range of neuropsychiatric conditions including disturbances in mood, sleep, and cognition [[44], [45], [46]]. Simply put, TNS is low level transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS) applied to the forehead, stimulating the nerve endings of the ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve that innervate the skin of the face. Motivated by the known coupling of the olfactory and trigeminal systems and the possibility of leveraging the trigeminal system to improve smell function, our group conducted a pilot trial of TNS in healthy, normosmic (normal sense of smell), adults [47]. Our findings indicated that a single, 30-min session of active TNS, compared to sham TNS, significantly improved the ability to detect odors for up to 30 min. Although the underlying mechanisms of TNS are not completely understood, and the durability of its effects beyond 30 min remain unknown, its observed short-term enhancement for the detection of odors may serve to prime the olfactory system for subsequent ST, thereby potentially augmenting the therapeutic benefits of ST. Moreover, in addition to the effects of TNS on olfaction that were noted in our study, some positive effects on mood and cognition were also demonstrated [47], suggesting that TNS may be an appropriate intervention for other Long COVID-related neuropsychiatric disturbances as well.

COVID exposed the critical need for evidence-based interventions to treat the collection of symptoms associated with Long COVID, and chemosensory dysfunction, in particular. Our ongoing clinical trial of ST and TNS will help to fill this gap in medicine. The current paper describes the study design and methodology of our home-based clinical trial designed to determine whether ST and/or TNS-enhanced ST improves Long COVID-related disturbances in sense of smell, mood, sleep, and cognition.

sec.title: Research objectives and hypotheses
sec.p: The primary objectives of this study are to assess ST for the treatment of persistent smell dysfunction and additional secondary symptoms of Long COVID after 12 weeks (primary endpoint), and to accelerate the benefits of ST through adjunctive TNS (4-week secondary endpoint). We will achieve these objectives with the following aims and hypotheses.

Aim 1: Determine the effects of ST on olfactory-specific deficits in Long COVID.Hypothesis 1a
ST compared to placebo ST (PBO) will demonstrate significantly greater improvement in psychophysical olfactory function at 12 weeks.Hypothesis 1b
ST compared to PBO will demonstrate significantly greater improvement in subjective olfactory function at 12 weeks.Hypothesis 1c
(secondary): Improvement in psychophysical function will correlate with improvement in subjective olfactory-specific function.Hypothesis 1d
(exploratory): The relationship between treatment intervention (ST vs PBO) and therapeutic outcome will be mediated (enabled) and/or moderated (amplified or abated) by clinical variables (e.g., baseline smell impairment, deficit in mood, attention, sleep, COVID history), demographic factors (e.g., age, sex), and/or procedural variables (e.g., treatment compliance, acceptability, and fidelity).

Aim 2: Determine the TNS-enhanced effects of ST on olfactory-specific deficits in Long COVID.Hypothesis 2a
TNS-enhanced ST compared to ST will demonstrate significantly greater improvement in psychophysical olfactory function at 4 weeks.Hypothesis 2b
TNS-enhanced ST compared to ST will demonstrate significantly greater improvement in subjective olfactory function at 4 weeks.Hypothesis 2c
(secondary): Improvement in psychophysical function will correlate with improvement in subjective olfactory-specific function.Hypothesis 2d
(exploratory): The relationship between treatment intervention (TNS-enhanced ST vs ST) and therapeutic outcome will be mediated (enabled) and/or moderated (amplified or abated) by clinical variables (e.g., baseline smell impairment, deficit in mood, attention, sleep, COVID history), demographic factors (e.g., age, sex), and/or procedural variables (e.g., treatment compliance, acceptability, and fidelity).

Aim 3: Determine if ST and/or TNS-enhanced ST improve other Long COVID symptoms, including deficits in mood, sleep, and cognitive function.Hypothesis 3a
ST compared to PBO will show a greater improvement in other Long COVID symptoms (i.e., mood, sleep and cognitive function) at 12 weeks.Hypothesis 3b
TNS-enhanced ST compared to ST will demonstrate significantly greater improvement in mood, cognition, and sleep at 4 weeks.Hypothesis 3c
The magnitude of treatment-induced improvements in psychophysical and subjective smell function will correlate with the magnitude of reduction in other Long COVID symptoms.
Additional Exploratory Aims: Explore feasibility, acceptability, fidelity and 24-week durability of ST and TNS-enhanced ST.

sec.title: Study design
sec.p:
Our study is a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial to assess the impact of ST for the treatment of post-COVID persistent smell dysfunction. This study will also assess the efficacy of TNS (single-blind, non-placebo controlled) as an adjunctive treatment to ST. Following initial phone screening, informed consent, rescreening to confirm eligibility, demographics and baseline assessment of smell, cognition, mood and sleep, participants are randomized (1:1:1), stratified by age, sex, and smell deficit, to ST, PBO, or TNS-enhanced ST. Participants self-administer their first training under the supervision of study staff and then train at-home twice per day, 5 days per week, for 12 weeks, a schedule consistent with previous ST treatment protocols [29,32]. Participants use Research Electronic Data Capture (REDCap) to record their daily training and complete biweekly Participant Reported Outcomes (PROs) [[73], [74]]. Participants return to the study site for follow-up assessments at the end of week 4 and 12 and complete follow-up PROs remotely, at week 24, using REDCap. Fig. 1 illustrates the study design.Fig. 1
Study design. PROs include Long COVID Symptom Survey (LCSS), Questionnaire of Olfactory Disorders – Negative Statements (QOD-NS), Impact of Olfactory Loss – Visual Analog Scale (IOL-VAS), Total Nasal Symptom Score (TNSS), Patient Global Impression of Severity/Change (PGI-S/C), Profile of Mood States – Short Form (POMS-SF), Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9), Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7 (GAD-7), Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI), Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS), Feasibility/Acceptability of Intervention Measure (FIM/AIM). Cognitive function assessment includes Sustained Attention Response Task (SART), Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), and National Institute of Health Toolbox-Cognitive Battery (NIHTB-CB).Fig. 1

sec.title: Ethics
sec.p: This study was reviewed and approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of the Medical University of South Carolina (MUSC; IRB # PRO127790) and carried out in accordance with The Code of Ethics of the World Medical Association (Declaration of Helsinki), as well as registered on ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05855369) prior to the recruitment of the first study participant. This study is monitored annually by a Data Safety Monitoring Board (DSMB). Participants provide informed consent prior to any study procedures and data collection taking place.

sec.title: Recruitment and screening
sec.p: Adult men and women (N = 180), of all races and ethnicities, seeking treatment for COVID-related persistent smell dysfunction are being recruited from the greater Charleston, South Carolina (SC) area through print and social media advertisements. Interested potential participants are emailed a synopsis of the study and their responsibilities as a participant and scheduled for a phone call with a study coordinator. During the call, the potential participant is given a full description of the study and has time for questions to be answered. If interested, the participant is screened for initial eligibility and sent a copy of the informed consent file to review. Those who meet initial inclusion/exclusion (I/E) criteria are scheduled to attend a baseline visit at the MUSC Department of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences.

sec.title: Participant eligibility
sec.p:
Once consented at the baseline visit, participants are re-screened. They must meet all I/E criteria and have the ability/willingness to complete all assessments and study procedures to be eligible to participate. Table 1 presents the I/E criteria.Table 1
Inclusion and exclusion criteria.Table 1
Inclusion Criteria
118–65 years old
2Confirmed COVID diagnosis
3COVID-related sudden smell loss, persisting at least 1 month
4Seeking treatment for their COVID-related sudden smell loss
5Naive to both ST and TNS
Exclusion Criteria
1History of head trauma
2Heavy cigarette use
3Pre-COVID sinonasal disease
4Neurologic/neurodegenerative disease
5Serious mental illness and/or suicidal ideation
6Intranasally-administered medications/drugs
7Steroids or immunomodulatory medications
8Psychotropic medication (unless a stable dose is maintained)

sec.title: Training odors
sec.p: Sixteen stock odorants are obtained from Burghart Messtechnik (Wedel, Germany) that include, group 1 - rose, clove, lemon, and eucalyptus; group 2 - cinnamon, peppermint, coffee, and orange; group 3 - lavender, vanilla, lilac, and ginger; and group 4 - chocolate, bacon, banana, and thyme. Odor delivery devices called Whispis™ (Scentovation™, Portland, ME) are purchased pre-labeled with the name and picture of each of the 16 stock odorants. Training odors, used for both ST and TNS-enhanced ST, are prepared for each participant by adding 150 μL of one of the 16 stock odorants to the corresponding labeled Whispi™. The training odors, chosen in part for their stability over time, were pilot tested with the Whispis™ prior to the start of the trial. Although found to be perceptually stable, the odorized Whispis™ are replaced at the 4-week visit to minimize potential change in the perceived quality over the course of the treatment period. Four Whispis™ labeled by number (i.e., 1, 2, 3 or 4) are prepared for PBO and contain 150 μL of low intensity rose or vanilla odorant (i.e., 0.0001 % dilution of stock odorant in mineral oil). These low intensity odorant concentrations, utilized in previous ST trials, are reliably detected by only 10 % of healthy volunteers [48]. The PBO Whispis™ are not replaced during the trial.

sec.title: Treatment cases
sec.p:
All-in-one treatment cases are utilized to make delivery of ST, PBO, and TNS-enhanced ST easy to administer in the at-home setting. The treatment case for ST contains 16 active odor training Whispis™ and an electronic tablet for any participant without access to a personal device. The treatment case for PBO contains 4 placebo Whispis™ and an electronic tablet (if needed), while the TNS-enhanced ST treatment case contains 16 active odor training Whispis™, a transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation device (InTENSity Twin Stim III, Compass Health Brands Corp., Middleburg Heights, OH), adhesive electrodes (Axelgaard PALS Blue Electrodes, Grayline Medical, Worwalk, CA), alcohol wipes, extra device batteries, and a headband to hold electrodes in place during stimulation. Instructional guides for respective training are also included (see Fig. 2).Fig. 2
TNS-enhanced ST treatment case.Fig. 2

sec.title: Assessments
sec.p: At each in-person visit, participants undergo psychophysical and subjective smell testing, a cognitive assessment, and then complete PROs, a battery of questionnaires and scales related to Long COVID symptoms, sense of smell, and severity of mood and/or sleep disturbance. All smell and cognitive assessments are conducted by a single, trained member of the study team who remains blinded to participants’ treatment assignments throughout the trial. Additional PROs related to treatment are completed at the follow-up visits (see Fig. 1). PROs are also completed remotely by REDCap at weeks 2, 6, 8, 10, and 24.

sec.title: Psychophysical smell testing (primary outcome measure)
sec.p: Sniffin’ Sticks (Burghart Messtechnik, Wedel, Germany) are utilized to determine olfactory dysfunction. Sniffin’ Sticks assess 3 functional subdomains of olfaction including threshold (T), discrimination (D), and identification (I). Each subdomain is scored 0 to 16 and then added for a composite TDI score (range 0–48), with higher scores indicating better olfaction and a cut-off score of ≥30.75 indicating normosmia, or a normal sense of smell.

sec.title: Subjective smell testing (key secondary outcome measures)
sec.p: Participants also rate suprathreshold concentrations of clove, rose, eucalyptol, banana, and peppermint (Burghart Messtechnik, Wedel, Germany) using 100 mm visual analog scales (VAS) to determine perceived intensity (anchor points: 0 = ”imperceptible” to 100 = “extremely intense”), hedonic valence (anchor points: 0 = “extremely unpleasant” to 100 = “extremely pleasant”), and whether the odors smell like they should (anchor points: 0 = “not at all” to 100 = “extremely”). Study staff administering the smell testing are blinded to treatment assignment.

sec.title: Cognitive testing (other secondary outcome measures)
sec.p: The Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA), a widely used paper and pencil screening tool that is sensitive to detecting cognitive impairment [49], especially in individuals with Long COVID [50,51], is used to determine deficits in global cognition. The Sustained Attention to Response Task (SART; Millisecond Software, LLC), a computerized go-no-go task, is used to evaluate sustained attention and inhibitory control in motor responses to frequent and rare stimuli. Through errors in commission and omission, as well as reaction times, the SART is a sensitive measure of attentional deficits across multiple patient populations with cognitive dysfunction [[52], [53], [54], [55]]. Five cognitive tests from the National Institute of Health Toolbox Cognitive Battery (NIHTB-CB), a standardized assessment tool used to evaluate cognitive function, are self-administered via iPad. These tests include 1) List Sorting Working Memory, a task that requires participants to recall and sort various items by size and category, 2) Flanker Inhibitory Control and Attention, a task requiring focus on a central stimulus while inhibiting attention to flanked stimuli, 3) Dimensional Change Card Sort, a task that requires participants to match bivalent pictures to a target picture based on either shape or color, 4) Pattern Comparison Processing Speed, a task during which participants determine whether two simultaneously presented pictures are the same or different, and 5) Picture Sequence Memory, an episodic memory task that requires participants to recall a sequence of items in the exact order they were presented. These NIHTB-CB tasks yield both individual and composite T-scores for fluid cognition. Study staff administering the cognitive assessments are blinded to treatment assignment.

sec.title: PROs (other secondary outcome measures)
sec.p: A battery of PROs is completed by participants using REDCap to determine baseline and treatment-related improvements in subjective olfactory function and other Long COVID symptoms including disturbances in mood and sleep. Olfactory-specific PROs include the Questionnaire of Olfactory Disorders-Negative Statements (QOD-NS), the Impact of Olfactory Loss Visual Analog Scale (IOL-VAS), and the Total Nasal Symptom Score (TNSS). The QOD-NS consists of 17 negative statements (scale from 0 to 3; total score from 0 to 51) and is reverse scored so that lower scores indicate less impairment in olfactory-related quality of life [56]. The IOL-VAS consists of 9 separate items assessing the impact of smell loss on mood, food enjoyment, social interactions, safety, hygiene, sex, cooking, appetite, and weight changes, rated from 0 (no impact) to 10 (biggest impact possible) [32]. The Total Nasal Symptom Score measures the severity of 4 common nasal symptoms including congestion/obstruction, runny nose/secretions, nasal itching and sneezing. Each item is scored on a scale from 0 (none) to 3 (severe), with a total score ranging from 0 to 12 [57]. The Patient Global Impression Severity and Change (PGI-S/C) are used to assess the participants’ judgment regarding the severity of smell loss as well as any potential change from baseline. The PGI-S ranges from 1 (normal, not at all affected) to 7 (extremely affected), while the PGI-C ranges from 1 (very much improved) to 7 (very much worse). Additional validated and reliable non-olfactory-specific PROs are used to determine severity of depression (Patient Health Questionnaire-9; PHQ-9) [58], anxiety (Generalized Anxiety Disorder-7; GAD-7) [59], mood disturbance (short form of the Profile of Mood States, POMS-SF) [60,61], daytime sleepiness (Epworth Sleepiness Scale; ESS) [62], and subjective sleep quality (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index; PSQI) [63].

sec.title: Feasibility, acceptability, and fidelity (exploratory outcome measures)
sec.p: Data on treatment feasibility, acceptability, and fidelity is also collected. Quantitative measures include the number of sessions completed (completion rate), technical problems, adverse and serious adverse events, and study drop-out. Qualitative measures include open-ended questions regarding treatment adherence (i.e., the treatment is being administered as intended, the nature of any technical problems, and the reasons for missed treatment sessions). Participants also complete the Feasibility of Intervention Measure (FIM) and the Acceptability of Intervention Measure (AIM) [64], both of which contain 4 items that are scored on the 5-point scale (1 = completely disagree to 5 = completely agree). Responses are averaged across the 4 items, with a score of 4 or more indicating adequate feasibility and acceptability of an intervention.

sec.title: Treatment interventions
sec.p: Participants receive detailed instructions on how to self-administer treatment and self-administer their first treatment under the supervision of study staff at the baseline visit. Participants leave the baseline visit with their treatment case and additional instructions for administering their daily at-home treatment.

sec.title: ST
sec.p: ST is self-administered by sniffing a group of 4 odorized Whispis™ in a random order for 10 s each. During sampling of each of the 4 odors, participants engage in active training by stating the name of the odor out loud, creating a visual representation of the odor (e.g., imagine pouring a cup of coffee), and using REDCap to rate the odor intensity, valence, and their ability to form the image/memory. Participants are instructed to randomize the order of training odors each day. The group of odorized Whispis™ are rotated each week, so that a total of 16 different odors are used over the course of the 12-week treatment period. Participants receive a daily REDCap link to record their training ratings or reason for the missed training. ST is self-administered twice per day for at least 5 days per week, totaling 120 training sessions over the 12-week treatment period.

sec.title: PBO
sec.p: PBO requires participants to smell the same 4, low-intensity, Whispis™, labeled 1–4, throughout the entire 12-week treatment period. Like ST, participants in PBO smell their Whispis™ in random order for 10 s each. However, they conduct passive smell training, meaning that no odor-related thoughts or tasks are performed while using the Whispis™. Participants receive an email to log their daily training on REDCap, but unlike ST, they do not provide daily ratings related to the training odors. PBO is also self-administered twice per day for at least 5 days per week, totaling 120 training sessions over the 12-week treatment period.

sec.title: TNS-enhanced ST
sec.p: TNS-enhanced ST requires participants to first prepare their skin by cleaning their forehead with an alcohol swab, attach two 4 cm × 4 cm square, conductive, hydrogel electrodes (Axelgaard PALS Blue Electrodes, Grayline Medical, Worwalk, CA) to their forehead just above each eyebrow, and place a headband over the electrodes to ensure proper attachment of the electrodes during the stimulation session. Once the electrodes are in place and attached to the TNS device (InTENSity Twin Stim III, Compass Health Brands Corp., Middleburg Heights, OH), participants turn the device on and increase the stimulation current to their threshold. Study staff determine each participants’ individual stimulation threshold at the beginning of the first treatment session by slowly increasing the level of current, 1 mA at a time, until the participant reports a mild sensation of tickling or vibration on their forehead or scalp. The current remains on this setting for the entire 30-min sessions, over the full 12-week treatment period. All other parameters on the TNS device including pulse rate = 120Hz, pulse width = 250μs, ramp = 5sec, and 50 % duty cycle (30s on, 30s off) are pre-set and unable to be changed by the participant. During TNS, participants sit and relax. The device is lightweight and portable, so movement can be achieved if necessary (e.g., bathroom break). The device beeps after 30 min, signaling that stimulation has ended and ST can begin. ST after TNS follows the same procedures as ST alone. TNS is self-administered once per day for at least 5 days per week, totaling 60 training sessions over the 12-week treatment period. This dose is within the range of therapeutic dose previously published for TNS [65,66]. Participants in the TNS-enhanced ST group self-administer ST twice on training days, once right after TNS and once on its own, totaling 120 ST sessions over the 12-week treatment period.

sec.title: Power and sample size
sec.p:
We based this analysis, in part, on olfactory-specific outcomes recently reported in a ST trial for COVID-related smell loss [67] and our previous pilot study of TNS [47]. In the ST study, improvements from baseline to 6-month follow-up, with an average of 3.5 months, were TDI: 24.7 (±8.9) to 30.9 (±9.8) and QOD-NS: 10.44 (±5.97) to 13.65 (±6.49). Under the assumption that these results are representative of ours, we will need NTDI = 20 and NQOD-NS = 31 study completers to establish statistical significance at α = 0.05 and (1-β) = 0.8. Our previous study assessing the acute effects of TNS demonstrated improved odor detection from −6.056 (1.262) to −6.806 (1.607). Assuming 12 weeks of the combined ST and TNS intervention produce at least this same effect, we estimate that NOD = 32 study completers will be sufficient to achieve statistical significance at α = 0.05 and (1-β) = 0.8. However, our power analysis was also designed for the high attrition/drop-out rates commonly reported in ST trials, some of which reached 40 %–45 % [32,67]. Allowing for up to 45 % attrition/drop-out, our target enrollment of N = 60 per treatment arm and N = 180 total (see Fig. 3) resulted in a sample size of N = 33 per treatment (N = 99 overall) required to achieve significant statistical power for our primary outcomes. Importantly, recruitment targets will be re-evaluated during the trial and adjusted if the observed attrition/drop-out differs significantly from the assumed rate of 45 %.Fig. 3
Flow diagram for participant recruitment, randomization, follow-up, and analytic process. ITT = intent to treat, PP = per protocol.Fig. 3

sec.title: Data analytic plan
sec.p: All data will be characterized using basic statistical descriptives (sample means, sample standard deviations, and higher moments; or frequencies or proportions for continuous and discrete outcome measures) and with graphical representations such as scatter plots, histograms, and box plots. Statistical independence of factors and covariates, homoscedasticity vs. heteroscedasticity, and sphericity (e.g., Mauchly's test) will be tested—particularly for repeated measures monitoring smell, cognition, and psychological functioning over time (baseline, week 4, week 12, and for biweekly PROs). Secondary demographic discriminators will be evaluated to inform decisions regarding treatment as explicit random factors in mixed models or as simple confounding variables in fixed-effect approaches. Non-parametric tests and mixed models will be implemented where applicable or needed. While linear relationships between predictors and outcome variables are expected, potential curvilinearity or generalized models will be explored. Statistical controls for multiple comparisons, such as Bonferroni or false discovery rate (FDR), will be applied where appropriate (e.g., secondary olfactory, mood and cognitive outcomes, etc.). IBM's SPSS v30 (www.ibm.com/analytics/us/en/technology/spss/), Python scripts and/or R (www.r-project.org) for statistical analyses, and Python and/or Matlab 2022b (MathWorks; mathworks.com) for data visualization, will be employed.

Analyses will be conducted on the complete cohort, the intent-to-treat sample (ITT analysis), and the subset consisting of all study participants that completed at least 75 % of ST and returned for the 4- and 12-week follow-up visits, the completer sample (PP analysis). Participants that do not complete the baseline assessment and in-person visits at 4 and 12 weeks will be considered dropouts. We expect an extremely low amount of missing data, <10 % equally distributed across the treatment arms with little impact on the overall findings. We will carefully assess whether partially missing data has a qualitative or significant quantitative effect on the overall analysis, and if conservative data imputation strategies are applicable. Common approaches to mitigate larger than 10 % of missing data, using propensity scores and Bayesian techniques with Markov Chain Monte Carlo to create imputation samples, may be feasible and will be considered. While we plan to exclude participants from the main analyses who have not completed at least 75 % of their ST sessions, we will exploratorily assess treatment compliance for all participants using the number of completed training sessions as a covariate (see H1d and H2d exploratory hypotheses in Table 2).Table 2
Overview of hypotheses and planned analyses.Table 2
HypothesisOutcome MeasureAnalysis Plan
H1a: ST vs. PBO will improve psychophysical smell function (T, D, I, and TDI) from baseline to 12 weeks.Changes in T, D, I, and TDI scoresDescriptive statistics, repeated measures analysis of variance (ANOVA), post hoc one-sided Student's t-tests (between-treatments & between-time points), non-parametric tests (as needed).
H1b: ST vs. PBO will improve subjective smell function (QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, and perception of suprathreshold odors) from baseline to 12 weeks.Changes in QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, TNSS and suprathreshold odor ratingsSame statistical methods as H1a.
H1c (Secondary): Improvement in psychophysical function will correlate with improvement in subjective smell function.Correlations between changes in TDI, QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, TNSS and suprathreshold odor ratingsScatter plots, Pearson/Kendall correlation, one-sample t-tests for correlation strength, independent-samples t-test (or Wilcoxon) to compare treatments.
H1d (Exploratory): Treatment outcome (ST vs. PBO) will be mediated or moderated by clinical, demographic, and/or procedural variables.Age, sex, COVID history, baseline deficits and severity, treatment complianceRegression models (e.g., Hayes' PROCESS package).
H2a: TNS-enhanced ST vs. ST will show greater improvement in psychophysical smell function at 4 and 12 weeks.Changes in T, D, I, and TDI scoresSame statistical methods as H1a.
H2b: TNS-enhanced ST vs. ST will show greater improvement in subjective smell function at 4 and 12 weeks.Changes in QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, TNSS and suprathreshold odor ratingsSame statistical methods as H1a.
H2c (Secondary): Improvement in psychophysical function will correlate with improvement in subjective olfactory function.Correlations between changes in TDI, QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, TNSS and suprathreshold odor ratingsSame statistical methods as H1c.
H2d (Exploratory): Treatment outcome (ST vs. TNS-enhanced ST) will be mediated or moderated by clinical, demographic, and/or procedural variables.Age, sex, COVID history, baseline deficits and severity, treatment complianceSame statistical methods as H1d.
H3a: ST vs. PBO will improve other Long COVID symptoms (i.e., mood, cognition, and sleep) at 12 weeks.Changes in PGI, POMS-SF, PHQ-9, GAD-7, PSQI, ESS, SART, MoCA, NIHTB-CBSame statistical methods as H1a.
H3b: TNS-enhanced ST vs. ST will show greater improvement in other Long COVID symptoms at 4 and 12 weeks.Changes in PGI, POMS-SF, PHQ-9, GAD-7, PSQI, ESS, SART, MoCA, NIHTB-CBSame statistical methods as H1a.
H3c: Improvement in smell function will correlate with a reduction in other Long COVID symptoms.Correlation between changes in TDI, QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, TNSS, suprathreshold odor ratings, PGI, POMS-SF, PHQ-9, GAD-7, PSQI, ESS, SART, MoCA, NIHTB-CBSame statistical methods as H1c.
Additional Exploratory Aims: Feasibility, acceptability, fidelity, and durability of interventions (ST & TNS-ST) at 24 weeks.Qualitative, AIM, FIM, and for durability (QOD-NS, IOL-VAS, TNSS, PGI, POMS-SF, PHQ-9, GAD-7, PSQI, ESS)Omnibus ANOVA, independent-samples t-tests (or Wilcoxon), and the same statistical methods as H1a for durability.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: COVID exposed the critical need for new and improved treatments for chemosensory dysfunction. To address this treatment gap, the present study is investigating the efficacy of ST and TNS-enhanced ST in the treatment of post-COVID persistent smell dysfunction and associated deficits in cognition, mood, and sleep. We hypothesize that both treatments will improve psychophysical and subjective smell function, but that treatment gains will be demonstrated in less time for TNS-enhanced ST compared to ST (4 vs 12 weeks).

A number of key considerations were addressed during the design and execution of this at-home clinical trial, particularly related to the eligibility and safety of participants, and in the maintenance of data integrity. Each decision involved a considerable amount of thought and discussion, balancing both scientific rigor with more practical matters. A brief overview of these decisions follows.

Participant eligibility based on COVID variant: Prevalence rates and severity of smell loss vary by COVID variant, with earlier, compared to the more recent, variants associated with greater deficits [68,69]. Limiting enrollment to those who lost their sense of smell during early COVID also reduces the potential confound that vaccination status may have on COVID-related persistent smell loss. While this approach will help to form a more homogeneous sample and reduce variability, a focus on early COVID has its own disadvantages. Not only will it reduce our recruitment potential and the generalizability of our findings, but it may negatively impact our results given that impairment of longer duration (>1 year) is associated with less improvement after ST [48]. Taking all into account, we decided to include all COVID-related persistent smell loss, regardless of wave/variant and collect a detailed COVID history, including documentation of diagnosis and vaccination status/dates. We will determine (via MUSC medical records) or estimate according to the dominant variant within the community at the time of illness (via the MUSC epidemiology project, https://web.musc.edu/coronavirus-updates/epidemiology-project), the wave/variant/s that caused (or likely caused) each participant's sudden and persistent smell loss. These variables will be included in the data analyses when appropriate.

Safety of TNS in remote trials: With approval by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for the at-home use in pediatric attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) [65,70] and adult acute migraine [71], TNS has an established safety profile as a self-administered treatment intervention in remote clinical trials. Importantly, the dose of TNS administered in the present study is below that approved for these other conditions. Yet, there are some safety concerns and potential side effects that require careful consideration when the independent administration of TNS is required, e.g., remote trials. The risks associated with TNS primarily include skin irritation, redness, or mild inflammation under the stimulating electrodes or mild discomfort at the stimulation site. This can occur when the current is too intense or applied incorrectly. We aim to significantly reduce these untoward effects with proper training that includes step-by-step instruction and in-person oversight during each participant's first treatment session. Additionally, we remind participants to contact us, in real-time, with any questions or concerns, as well as any adverse reactions or problems with the TNS device. All adverse events/serious adverse events (AEs/SAEs) will be reported to the study physicians and the DSMB within 24 h and to MUSC's IRB as soon as possible but within 72 h for a non-fatal or non-life-threatening event and within 24 h for a fatal or life-threatening event. MUSC's IRB will evaluate all AEs/SAEs and determine what additional steps, if any, are needed to manage the event (e.g., change in protocol, suspension). Greater than minor skin irritation or inflammation at the electrode site that does not resolve or worsens with each treatment, as well as discomfort or pain that persists between sessions will be considered AEs that may warrant treatment discontinuation.

Treatment compliance: Knowing that 12 weeks of daily ST can be burdensome and lead to study fatigue and drop-out, we carefully considered strategies that would help to increase treatment compliance, without being overly burdensome. The choice of using REDCap during training in the current trial offers several key advantages in this respect. Participants are reminded to complete their daily training via an automated link emailed each day to their smartphone or study device. Using each link provides a time-stamped record of that day's training, including data specific to the participant's experience with the training odors that day. Treatment compliance, including the time of day a participant typically trains and individual odor ratings, is remotely monitored by study staff. Any irregularities in training can be addressed in real-time with participants. Additionally, if a training day is missed, a new link is sent for participants to record their reason for missed training, and if consecutive days of training are missed, study staff can address this issue with participants in a timely manner. Using REDCap during training was found to increase training time/burden by less than 5 min, while the benefits it provides in study retention, treatment integrity, and timely correction are invaluable.

Limitations: The proposed study has several limitations that should be acknowledged. The first relates to the generalizability of the findings. Our strict I/E criteria are aimed at minimizing the potential influence of confounding factors on smell loss, e.g., an upper age limit to help reduce the influence of age-related loss. We acknowledge that the focus on COVID-related smell loss, as well as our single-site design with recruitment limited to the greater Charleston, SC area may restrict the generalizability of our findings to the broader population. Additional limitations concern the lack of a TNS-only treatment arm, the relatively short follow-up period of 24 weeks, which limits the ability to evaluate long-term durability of ST and TNS, and the use of PROs that can be influenced by recall and reporting biases. Future investigations that expand recruitment across multiple sites, include more etiologically heterogenous cohorts, have longer follow-up periods and utilize additional objective outcomes will be critical for determining the efficacy of ST and TNS in broader contexts. Additionally, placebo-controlled trials will be necessary to establish the independent efficacy and optimal dosing parameters required for the development of TNS as a stand-alone therapy for smell loss. Positive findings from such studies, combined with the low cost, ease of administration, and established safety of TNS, indicate that it is readily scalable and well-suited for broad clinical implementation. Our proposed study, along with future investigations, will lay the groundwork for translational research aimed at determining how TNS can be effectively integrated into routine care.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: According to a 2022 National Health Interview Survey, 6.9 % of adults reported having Long COVID [72], while other studies reported a much greater percentage (∼54 %) endorsed at least 1 long-term symptom of COVID [8]. These numbers translate into tens, if not hundreds, of millions of people throughout the world that may be living with symptoms Long COVID. Yet few evidence-based interventions exist to treat the neuropsychiatric symptoms associated with this condition. To help fill this treatment gap, we designed a study that utilizes safe, home-based interventions (ST and TNS) that, combined, have very high potential to show efficacy in treating COVID-related persistent smell loss and other symptoms of Long COVID. Positive findings from this study will provide the foundation for the further development and widespread study of ST and TNS, which may extend beyond COVID.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Nicole Cash: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Mary Clare Koebel: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation. Bashar W. Badran: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aicko Y. Schumann: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lisa M. McTeague: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Thomas W. Uhde: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Rodney J. Schlosser: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Bernadette M. Cortese: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: No data was used for the research described in the article.

fn.p: This publication was supported, in part, by the National Institute on Deafness and Other Communication Disorders (DC021179) and the National Center for Advancing Translational Sciences (UL1 TR001450) of the National Institutes of Health (NIH). Study sponsors had no role in the study design and methodology, in the writing of this report, or in the decision to submit it for publication. All authors contributed to the manuscript composition and illustrations and approved its final form. All authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.
